name: Iridium
name_origin: From Latin 'iris', meaning 'rainbow', because many of iridium's salts are strikingly multicolored. Named by its discoverer Smithson Tennant in 1803.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Iridium is one of the rarest elements in Earth's crust (~0.001 ppm) but is more abundant in many meteorites; the iridium anomaly in the Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary clay was a key piece of evidence for the asteroid impact theory of the dinosaur extinction. Most iridium is recovered as a byproduct of platinum and nickel mining in South Africa and Russia.
states: elevated in iron meteorites and the K-Pg boundary clay

compounds.name: Iridium dioxide
compounds.summary: A blue-black rutile-structured oxide that is an outstanding electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution in PEM water electrolysis.

compounds.name: Iridium trichloride
compounds.summary: An olive-green solid used as a precursor for iridium organometallic catalysts and OLED emitters.

compounds.name: Tris(2-phenylpyridine)iridium
compounds.summary: A cyclometalated complex used as a green phosphorescent emitter in OLED displays.

compounds.name: Iridium hexafluoride
compounds.summary: A yellow volatile solid that is one of the most powerful fluorinating agents known.

compounds.name: Hexachloroiridate(IV)
compounds.summary: A dark red anion used in electrochemistry as a stable one-electron outer-sphere oxidant.

uses: Crucibles for growing high-purity oxide single crystals (sapphire, YAG) at high temperatures
Spark-plug electrodes in high-performance and aviation engines
Anodes (IrO2-RuO2) for chlorine and oxygen evolution in industrial electrolysis
Catalysts and emissive complexes in phosphorescent OLED displays and lighting
Defining the international standard kilogram and meter (historically, Pt-Ir alloy)
X-ray and gamma-ray sources (192Ir) for industrial radiography and brachytherapy
reactivity_summary: Iridium is the second-densest element (~22.56 g/cm3) and the most corrosion-resistant metal known. It is unattacked by all acids and aqua regia at room temperature, and resists molten salts and silicates better than any other metal, which is why it is used for high-temperature crucibles. It can be slowly attacked by molten alkali cyanides and by oxygen at red heat. Compact iridium is essentially inert in air up to 1000 C.
isotopes_summary: Iridium has two naturally occurring isotopes: 191Ir (~37.3%) and 193Ir (~62.7%), both stable. The synthetic isotope 192Ir (t1/2 ~73.8 d) is widely used as a gamma source for industrial radiography and as a sealed source in high-dose-rate brachytherapy for cancer treatment. The 193mIr isomer is used in Mossbauer spectroscopy.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
